3.1.label: Question 3.1
3.1.scenario:
Comment briefly on the following piece of advice:

‘Buying an immediate needs long‐term care annuity to provide an income to cover the cost of care for the rest of your life is foolish. These contracts will be backed with fixed‐interest securities and so you would do better to buy these directly and cut out the insurer’s expenses and profit. Also, when you die there will be the added advantage that you can bequeath the money left over to your relatives and friends, rather than to the insurance company.’

Comment on Advice: The principal objection to this comment concerns the absence of insurance if someone were to take this advice. [1]
An annuity may be thought of as insurance against the financial consequences of living too long (ie beyond the point at which your money runs out). [1]
Without an annuity, the individual is taking the risk of living too long (longevity risk). [1]
An individual that needs long-term care will not be in a position to supplement their income when their savings run out. [1]
In fact, rather than leaving money to your relatives and friends, you may need to rely on them to help pay for your care. [1]
Running out of money may mean that you have to change your lifestyle (eg move to a cheaper care home) at a time when you are least able to adapt to change. [1]
It might also be worth mentioning that:
the insurer’s administrative expenses and profit would be cut out (assuming that the insurer is making a profit) [½]
the expenses of buying fixed-interest investments directly may be higher for the individual than the insurer [½]
the individual might not know which are the best securities to buy [½]

the contract might not be backed entirely with fixed-interest securities, eg if the company has sufficient investment freedom to invest a proportion of the funds in equities, index-linked gilts or corporate bonds, it may be able to offer better rates to policyholders. [½]

3.2.label: Question 3.2
3.2.scenario:
Alice is aged 78 and she has recently had a stroke. This has resulted in her needing to move into a nursing home.

She has £7,000 of net income a year from her State pension and a small occupational pension. The nursing home chosen by Alice charges fees of £25,000 per year. Alice therefore needs an extra £18,000 pa net income.

She has £75,000 from her savings and from the sale of her property.

You can assume that:
• a stroke increases the initial (q‐type) mortality rate by + 0.35 in the year following the stroke, and by + 0.10 in all other subsequent years
• normal mortality follows ELT15
• the appropriate interest rate to use is 5% pa effective.

(i).question: Describe and quantify the risk faced by Alice.

Risks faced by Alice: Alice has an immediate need to meet residual care costs of £18,000 pa. [½]
Her only resource is capital of £75,000. [½]
Invested at 5% this capital might produce an annual income of £3,750 pa, which is far short of her expected annual care costs. [½]
If she uses her capital to fund her care costs she runs the risk of living too long, so that her capital is exhausted. [1]
Solving the equation: 75,000 = 18,000 * ä_n@5% suggests her capital will run out in about 4.75 years. [1]
Using ELT15 (Females) mortality as normal mortality we have q'78 = 0.398, q'79 = 0.154, q'80 = 0.160, q'81 = 0.166, q'82 = 0.172. [½]
So there is a probability of approximately 0.3 that Alice will live for 5 years or more. [½]

(ii).question: Using the information given, suggest an immediate needs solution to the risk faced by Alice. Justify your suggestion.

Immediate needs solution: Suggest an impaired life annuity (immediate needs annuity). [1]
We can do some rough calculations to determine the cost of an impaired life annuity. [½]
Using the probabilities calculated, ä'78 is approximately 3.75. [1]
The insurer will need to allow for expenses and profits (say a loading of 10%). [½]
So the net purchase price of the impaired life annuity might be such that a lump sum of £10,000 buys an annuity of £2,425 pa. [1]
A capital sum of £75,000 would buy an immediate impaired life annuity of about £18,200 pa. [1]
This removes the 'risk of living too long' from Alice’s life and provides a solution to paying for her care costs. [½]
There may be the possibility of buying an annuity with a modest rate of annual increase to take account of inflation in care costs. [½]
